items.q: Why is high IQ a problem for the statistical infrequency definition?
items.correct: It is statistically rare but not abnormal/undesirable.
items.misconception: Because high IQ is actually common.
items.why: Students miss that statistical infrequency lacks a value judgement - it just measures rarity. High IQ is rare but desirable, exposing the definition's limitation.

items.q: Which part of the psyche operates on the reality principle?
items.why: Students confuse ego (reality) with superego (morality). The ego mediates between id's demands and reality's constraints. The superego is the internalised moral conscience.

items.q: In Milgram's study, what percentage of participants gave the maximum 450V shock?
items.why: Students remember 'most' obeyed and assume all did. Actually 65% went to 450V; 35% defied. This nuance matters because it shows obedience is strong but not absolute.

items.q: Generalising stress research done on men to all humans is an example of what?
items.why: It's Beta bias. “Alpha bias” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Studying behaviour from inside a single culture is called what?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Emic. “Etic” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year was Ainsworth's Strange Situation published?
items.why: The real figure is 1978. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
